notes.note: Quintus's much-trusted (and notorious) freedman, often Q.fr. correspondence's offstage figure; Cicero teases that nobody in the brothers' circle is more apt to interrupt him than Quintus or Statius.

Asicius was a friend of Caelius and a partisan of Pompey; the litter once belonged to Ptolemy XII Auletes, who used Asicius's hospitality and entourage during his stay in Italy. The eight bearers and hundred sword-men are the marks of a quasi-royal escort.
The mountain near Athens, by metonymy a sign of Athenian (Academic, Stoic) learning; Cicero's joke is that his philosophy is rough-and-ready Italian, not polished Greek.
Sophocles's hero, abandoned on Lemnos with a festering wound; here a figure for the wronged citizen who watches the spectacles that grieve him.
